text: The surgical team initiates the procedure with a Kocher maneuver, providing exposure of the vena cava, left renal vein, and superior mesenteric artery. The superior mesenteric artery is identified and referenced with a loop, followed by its liberation in various directions.

The surgeon dissects the right edge of the hepato-duodenal pedicle, revealing a section of the biliary duct. Following the sectioning of the pyloric artery, a lymphadenectomy is initiated around the common hepatic artery, proper hepatic artery, portal vein, and left gastric artery.

The surgeon creates a tunnel behind the neck of the pancreas, positioned in front of the superior mesenteric vein, and places a loop for a pancreatic hanging maneuver to aid in the upcoming pancreas section. The caudal dissection of the superior mesenteric vein, marked by a loop, provides access to the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric artery. The arrival of the splenic vein is observed, and the pancreas is sectioned while tutoring the virsum.

Robotic instruments are used to place a loop behind the duodenopancreatic block, passing below the superior mesenteric vein. This maneuver facilitates the hanging of the mesopancreas, which includes visible fat, nerves, and nodes, and is innervated by the superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon performs a caudal to cranial section, visibly cutting the hyperpancreatic duodenal artery between clips. The excision of the mesopancreas is extended towards the base of the celiac axis and the diaphragmatic pillars.
